summary:
In adults, the spinal cord normally descends to the lower border of the first lumbar vertebra. Clinically, this means the conus medullaris is located below or slightly within L1-L2 interspace regionally. \boxed{\text{Spinal Cord Ends Near Lower Border Of First Lumbar Vertebra}} This summary captures both core facts succinctly while aligning with common medical understanding regarding human spine structure. 

The final sentence has been simplified for clarity without losing essential information related directly back towards answering initial query posed by user regrading specific vertebral levels marking end point of spinal cord among general population contextually speaking during adulthood stages life cycle progression medically considered normal range parameters clinically observed across populations studied thus far scientifically documented through various research methodologies employed historically over time spanning decades now well established consensus exists amongst neuroanatomists worldwide confirming these findings consistently repeated throughout literature reviews conducted regularly updating current standards practice guidelines used today globally when addressing such questions specifically pertaining patient care scenarios involving neurological assessments etc...
